articles.bodyText: Mercury Poisoning: Symptoms, Causes, and How to Check For Heavy Metal Exposure Low-level mercury exposure is common over time and can lead to ongoing health problems April 30, 2026 · Biomarkers Order mercury testing from $53. Schedule online, results in a week Clear guidance, follow-up care available HSA/FSA Eligible Start testing Learn more Low-level mercury exposure is common over time and can lead to ongoing health problems Many people with brain fog, fatigue, mood changes, or odd neurological symptoms don’t know that heavy metal exposure might be a cause. In fact, 20 to 30% of adults have detectable levels of heavy metals in their bodies , like mercury. Mercury is a toxic heavy metal that can enter the body through food, the environment, or occupational exposure. It causes symptoms that may overlap with other conditions. A heavy metal test, such as a mercury screening, is the most reliable way to assess exposure levels and guide next steps. What is Mercury Poisoning? Mercury poisoning happens when mercury builds up in the body. This can disrupt normal cell and brain function. Since it has no biological role in the human body, mercury can build up in tissues over time, particularly in the brain, kidneys, and nervous system. The most common sources of exposure include: Large predatory fish (such as tuna, swordfish, and king mackerel) Industrial emissions or contaminated environments Certain occupational exposures Rare environmental contamination cases Even low-level exposure over long periods may contribute to subtle adverse health effects. Mercury Poisoning Symptoms Mercury exposure can affect the lungs, kidneys, brain, and skin. These are the common symptoms of mercury poisoning : 1. Respiratory symptoms Inhaling mercury vapor can cause coughing, chest tightness, or a burning sensation. It may also cause respiratory distress (dyspnea). Some people might have fluid buildup in the lungs (pulmonary edema), pneumonia, or scarring of lung tissue (fibrosis). 2. Neurological and Cognitive Symptoms Mercury primarily affects the nervous system, leading to various neurological and cognitive symptoms. Many of the earliest signs of mercury poisoning include brain fog, difficulty concentrating, memory issues, slower thinking, and mental fatigue. Mood changes such as irritability or anxiety, as well as headaches and hand tremors, are also common. These neurological and emotional symptoms often develop gradually and may be mistaken for stress or burnout. 3. Sensory and Physical Symptoms Mercury can significantly impact sensory processing and nerve function, leading to the following symptoms. Individuals may experience tingling or numbness in their hands, feet, or face, as well as blurred vision. Light or sound sensitivity and a metallic taste in the mouth can also occur. Awareness of these effects is vital for recognizing mercury exposure. 4. Digestive and Systemic Symptoms In some cases, mercury exposure may contribute to general systemic discomfort, leading to various symptoms. Individuals may experience a loss of appetite, nausea, or mild digestive upset. You may also feel constantly tired and weak. This further impacts your overall well-being. 5. Kidney and Detox-Related Effects Long-term mercury exposure can significantly affect kidney function and detoxification processes, because the kidneys filter mercury. Signs that mercury is affecting the kidneys include unexplained fatigue, fluid retention or mild swelling, and changes in urinary patterns. Why Symptoms Alone Are Not Enough Many symptoms of mercury poisoning mimic common conditions, such as nutrient deficiencies, stress, thyroid imbalance, or chronic fatigue. This is why testing is essential for clarity. How to Test for Mercury Exposure The only reliable way to confirm exposure is through laboratory testing. A mercury test evaluates mercury levels in the body using various biological samples. Types of Mercury Testing 1. Blood Test (Recent Exposure) A blood test for mercury poisoning shows recent exposure. It’s best for checking short-term or recent intake. It is especially helpful after eating a lot of seafood. It shows the mercury levels currently in the blood. Blood mercury levels might not show long-term exposure to the element. 2. Urine Test (Longer-Term Exposure) Urine testing is commonly used to assess ongoing exposure to metallic elements, including mercury. It measures the mercury excreted by the body. This makes it good for spotting long-term exposure patterns. This testing is useful in job screenings. It often goes with chelation therapy, especially provoked testing, to better find mercury levels. 3. Hair Analysis (Long-Term Exposure Pattern) Hair testing provides a longer-term exposure profile to mercury. It reflects exposure over weeks to months and is often used in environmental exposure assessments. This method shows exposure patterns instead of just acute levels. This helps understand a person’s mercury exposure history over time. Heavy Metals Test At Mito Health, we offer a Heavy Metals Test designed to help you understand your exposure levels and take proactive steps toward better health. The test provides insight into key heavy metals, including mercury, and supports a preventive approach to long-term wellness. A complete heavy metals test also checks other toxic elements such as lead and arsenic. This provides a broader picture of environmental exposure, not just mercury alone. How to Interpret Heavy Metal Blood Test Results A heavy metal blood test measures the amount of toxic metals circulating in your bloodstream. It reflects recent or ongoing exposure. Based on clinical data and population studies, mercury levels can be interpreted in tiers: Tier Mercury Level (Blood) Approx. µg/L What It Means Baseline / Typical ≤ 23 nmol/L ≤ 4.6 µg/L Within the expected range for most people. Reflects background environmental exposure. Mild Elevation 24, 49 nmol/L ~5, 9.9 µg/L Slightly above the population average. Often linked to regular fish intake or mild exposure. Moderate Elevation 50, 199 nmol/L 10, 39 µg/L Higher-than-average exposure. May indicate ongoing intake or accumulation. High / Action Level ≥ 200 nmol/L ≥ 40 µg/L Significantly elevated. Above typical environmental exposure levels. Most lab reports compare your results to a reference range based on population averages. For best results, interpret your heavy metals test alongside other health markers and retest after making changes. Why Heavy Metal Testing Matters for Prevention Heavy metal testing is important for detecting hidden exposure before serious symptoms arise. Mercury buildup can negatively affect brain function, energy production, oxidative stress, and long-term neurological health. Early detection empowers individuals to make informed lifestyle and dietary changes. How to Reduce Mercury Exposure Naturally While testing provides clarity, prevention starts with reducing heavy metal exposure and supporting natural detox pathways. Dietary awareness: Be mindful of your diet by opting for low-mercury fish such as salmon, sardines, and anchovies, while limiting the intake of larger predatory fish. Nutritional support from selenium-rich foods: Brazil nuts, along with antioxidant-rich fruits, vegetables, and protein. Gut and liver support: High-fiber foods and proper hydration can enhance the body’s ability to eliminate toxins. These approaches support the body’s natural ability to process and reduce heavy metal toxins . Heavy Metals Testing for Mercury Exposure Mercury exposure is often silent, slow, and difficult to detect based on symptoms alone. Many mercury poisoning symptoms overlap with everyday health issues, making testing the most reliable way to gain clarity. A heavy metals test helps determine whether exposure levels are within a safe range or require further attention. articles.description: Learn what a metabolic panel measures, including glucose levels, ranges, and how to understand your metabolic blood test results.
articles.bodyText: Metabolic Panel Explained: What Your Blood Test Means A metabolic panel is one of the most commonly ordered lab tests and one of the most misunderstood. April 29, 2026 · Biomarkers Order metabolic testing from $36. Schedule online, results in a week Clear guidance, follow-up care available HSA/FSA Eligible Start testing Learn more A metabolic panel is one of the most commonly ordered lab tests and one of the most misunderstood. Has your care team lead ever ordered you to get your metabolic levels checked? A metabolic panel test result may seem simple, but it reveals how your body handles energy, fluids, and nutrients. Your metabolism turns food into energy. It also helps control blood sugar and keeps different systems in balance. A metabolic blood test tracks these processes in real time. It often shows early signs of imbalance before symptoms appear. Knowing your glucose levels, electrolytes, and kidney markers helps you make better health choices. What Is a Metabolic Panel? A metabolic panel is a group of blood tests that measures key chemicals in your bloodstream. These markers reflect how well your body regulates energy, hydration, and organ function. There are two main types: Basic Metabolic Panel (BMP): The BMP includes eight core markers that assess glucose balance, kidney function, and electrolyte status. Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (CMP): It includes all the BMP markers plus liver enzymes and proteins, giving a broader overview of your health. What Does a Basic Metabolic Panel Measure? A basic metabolic panel focuses on eight essential markers. Each one plays a role in your overall metabolic health . 1. Glucose Levels Glucose is the body’s main source of energy, and even mildly elevated levels over time can indicate metabolic dysfunction. The normal fasting glucose range is approximately 70 to 99 mg/dL. High glucose levels may suggest insulin resistance or an increased risk of diabetes. Low glucose levels could indicate hypoglycemia, a condition that requires medical attention. 2. Sodium Sodium helps regulate fluid balance and nerve function in the body. The normal sodium level range is about 135-145 mmol/L. High sodium levels usually mean dehydration or too much salt. Low levels can show problems, such as fluid imbalance or hormonal issues. 3. Potassium Potassium supports muscle contractions and maintains heart rhythm. The normal potassium level in the body is about 3.5-5.0 mmol/L. High levels, known as hyperkalemia, can lead to serious heart problems and kidney issues. Low levels, or hypokalemia, can cause muscle weakness and an irregular heartbeat. 4. Chloride Chloride works in conjunction with sodium to help maintain fluid balance in the body. The normal chloride level range is about 95, 105 mmol/L. High chloride levels may mean dehydration or a metabolic issue. Low levels can happen from vomiting or losing fluids. 5. Bicarbonate (CO₂) Bicarbonate helps maintain the body’s acid-base balance, with normal levels between 23-30 mmol/L. High bicarbonate levels can signal metabolic alkalosis, and low levels may point to metabolic acidosis. 6. Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) Blood Urea Nitrogen (BUN) is a measure of the urea nitrogen in the blood, a waste product that the kidneys normally filter out. The normal range for BUN levels is approximately 8-21 mg/dL. Elevated BUN levels may indicate conditions such as kidney dysfunction, dehydration, or high protein intake. In contrast, low levels are less common but could be related to liver disease or malnutrition. 7. Creatinine Creatinine is an important indicator of kidney filtration efficiency, with a normal range typically between 0.5-1.1 mg/dL for women and 0.7-1.3 mg/dL for men. High creatinine levels may indicate impaired kidney function. Low levels often relate to reduced muscle mass. 8. Calcium Calcium is essential for the health of bones, muscles, and nerve signaling. The normal range for calcium levels in the blood is approximately 8.6-10.2 mg/dL. High calcium levels can stem from hormonal issues or too much supplementation. Low levels might point to a vitamin D deficiency or gaps in your diet. Beyond the Basic Panel: Comprehensive Metabolic Markers A basic metabolic panel provides a snapshot, but it does not fully capture how your metabolism is functioning over time. Advanced markers can show early metabolic issues. They often relate to insulin sensitivity and long-term blood sugar control. 1. Fasting Insulin Fasting insulin measures the amount of insulin your body produces after fasting. The optimal range for fasting insulin is approximately 2-6 µIU/mL. Levels outside this range, particularly high levels, may suggest insulin resistance, even if blood glucose levels are normal. 2. HbA1c HbA1c , or Glycated Hemoglobin, is a test that reflects the average blood sugar levels over the past two to three months. The normal range for HbA1c is less than 5.7%. A result between 5.7% and 6.4% is considered elevated and falls within the prediabetes range, while a level of 6.5% or higher is indicative of diabetes. 3. Estimated Average Glucose (eAG) EAG translates HbA1c levels into daily glucose averages, providing a clearer picture of a person’s blood sugar management. The normal range for eAG is 70-100 mg/dL, indicating healthy glucose levels. High eAG levels show ongoing high blood sugar. This can lead to health concerns. 4. HOMA-IR (Insulin Resistance Score) HOMA-IR is a score that estimates insulin resistance by using measurements of glucose and insulin levels. A score less than 1.0 indicates healthy insulin sensitivity, while scores between 1.0 and 1.9 suggest early resistance. A score of 2.0 or higher indicates insulin resistance, which can impair metabolic health. 5. Uric Acid Uric acid is a waste product that is a byproduct of purine metabolism in the body. The normal range for uric acid levels is typically 2.4-6.0 mg/dL for women and 3.4-7.0 mg/dL for men. When levels exceed these thresholds, it can show an increased risk of metabolic syndrome and gout. 6. Ferritin Ferritin indicates how much iron the body stores and the body’s inflammatory level. Normal levels usually range from 30-300 ng/mL, but this can differ by sex. High levels can show inflammation or metabolic issues. Low levels usually mean iron deficiency. Full Metabolic Panel Markers and Ranges Marker Normal Range Low Levels May Indicate High Levels May Indicate Glucose 70, 99 mg/dL Hypoglycemia, poor energy balance Insulin resistance, diabetes risk Sodium 135, 145 mmol/L Fluid imbalance, adrenal issues Dehydration, high salt intake Potassium 3.5, 5.0 mmol/L Muscle weakness, irregular heartbeat Kidney issues, heart rhythm problems Chloride 95, 105 mmol/L Fluid loss, vomiting Dehydration, metabolic imbalance Bicarbonate 23, 30 mmol/L Metabolic acidosis Metabolic alkalosis BUN 8, 21 mg/dL Liver issues, low protein intake Kidney stress, dehydration Creatinine 0.5, 1.1 mg/dL (women) 0.7, 1.3 mg/dL (men) Low muscle mass Reduced kidney function Calcium 8.6, 10.2 mg/dL Vitamin D deficiency, low intake Hormonal imbalance, excess intake Fasting Insulin 2, 6 µIU/mL optimal range Reduced insulin production Insulin resistance HbA1c <5.7% Rare; possible hypoglycemia Prediabetes, diabetes EAG 70, 100 mg/dL Low average glucose Chronic high glucose HOMA-IR <1.0 optimal range High insulin sensitivity (generally favorable) Insulin resistance Uric Acid 2.4, 6.0 mg/dL (women) 3.4, 7.0 mg/dL (men) Rare; possible liver or kidney issues Metabolic syndrome, gout risk Ferritin 30, 300 ng/mL Iron deficiency Inflammation, iron overload Understanding Your Metabolic Test Results On your metabolic panel report , you’ll see if your results are in a “normal” range. But there’s an important distinction to understand: clinical ranges are not the same as optimal ranges. Clinical Ranges Clinical ranges are based on population averages. They represent the values within which most people fall, typically about 95% of the general population. These ranges are useful for ide

articles.bodyText: Mthfr Gene Mutation What It Means for Folate B12 and Your Health You eat well. You take your vitamins. You do everything right—yet something still feels off. Fatigue you can't shake. Brain fog that clouds your mornings. Mood dips that don't quite make sense. What if the answer wasn't in your habits, but in your genes? April 20, 2026 · Biomarkers Order homocysteine testing from $44. Schedule online, results in a week Clear guidance, follow-up care available HSA/FSA Eligible Start testing Learn more You eat well. You take your vitamins. You do everything right, yet something still feels off. Fatigue you can’t shake. Brain fog that clouds your mornings. Mood dips that don’t quite make sense. What if the answer wasn’t in your habits, but in your genes? A common genetic variation called the MTHFR gene mutation affects roughly 40, 60% of the population, and most people have never heard of it. It doesn’t mean you’re broken. It means your body processes certain nutrients differently, and once you understand that, you can work with your biology instead of against it. In this article, you’ll learn what the MTHFR mutation actually is, how it disrupts folate and B12 metabolism, what symptoms it can cause, and what you can do to optimize your health with this knowledge. What Is the MTHFR Gene Mutation? MTHFR stands for methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase. It’s a mouthful, but the concept is straightforward. This gene provides instructions for producing an enzyme that plays a central role in processing folate (vitamin B9) and regulating a process called methylation. When there’s a variant in this gene, that enzyme works less efficiently. The two most studied variants are C677T and A1298C. The C677T variant, in particular, has been extensively researched. Carrying one copy (heterozygous) reduces enzyme activity by about 35%. Carrying two copies (homozygous) can reduce it by up to 70% [1]. That’s a significant difference in how your body handles some essential nutrients. Having an MTHFR mutation isn’t a diagnosis or a disease. It’s a genetic variation, one that interacts with your diet, lifestyle, and environment. Understanding it gives you the power to make informed, targeted choices about your health. How Does MTHFR Affect Folate and Methylation? To understand why MTHFR matters, you need a quick look at methylation. Methylation is a biochemical process that happens billions of times per second in every cell of your body. It’s involved in DNA repair, detoxification, neurotransmitter production, immune function, and gene expression. It serves as your body’s master regulatory system. The MTHFR enzyme converts folate into its active form: 5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF). This active folate is essential for donating methyl groups throughout the body. When MTHFR isn’t working at full capacity, your cells receive less of this active folate, even if you’re eating plenty of leafy greens or taking a standard folic acid supplement [2]. What most people don’t realize is that synthetic folic acid (found in most multivitamins and fortified foods) requires conversion by the MTHFR enzyme to become usable. If your enzyme activity is reduced, unmetabolized folic acid can accumulate in your bloodstream, which research suggests may have its own downstream effects [2]. This is why the form of folate you consume matters enormously when you have an MTHFR variant. The Link Between MTHFR, B12, and Homocysteine MTHFR doesn’t work in isolation. It’s part of a tightly connected nutrient network that includes vitamin B12 , folate , and an amino acid called homocysteine . When methylation is impaired, homocysteine (a byproduct of protein metabolism) can accumulate in the blood instead of being recycled back into beneficial compounds. Elevated homocysteine levels are one of the most well-studied consequences of MTHFR dysfunction. Research shows that high homocysteine is associated with increased cardiovascular risk, cognitive decline, and inflammation [3]. A landmark study in Circulation found that individuals with the homozygous C677T variant had significantly higher homocysteine levels compared to those without the mutation [4]. B12 is critical in this process. Vitamin B12, specifically in its active methylcobalamin form, works alongside active folate to convert homocysteine back into methionine, a beneficial amino acid. If you’re low in B12, or if your body isn’t converting it to its active form efficiently, the entire cycle slows down. Checking both B12 and homocysteine as biomarkers gives you a far more complete picture than looking at either one alone. What Symptoms Are Associated with MTHFR Variants? MTHFR variants don’t cause symptoms directly, but impaired methylation and elevated homocysteine can contribute to a wide range of concerns over time. Because methylation touches so many systems, the effects can feel diffuse and hard to pin down. That’s often why it goes unrecognized for years. Common experiences reported by people with MTHFR variants include: Persistent fatigue that doesn’t improve with rest Brain fog, difficulty concentrating, or memory lapses Mood imbalances, including anxiety or low mood Sensitivity to alcohol or certain medications History of pregnancy complications or recurrent miscarriage Slow recovery from illness or high susceptibility to infections These symptoms have many possible causes. An MTHFR variant is one piece of the puzzle, not a guaranteed explanation. But if you’ve been chasing answers without clear results, understanding your genetic profile, alongside your actual biomarker levels, can be the missing context you need. Who Should Consider Testing for MTHFR? MTHFR genetic testing is becoming more accessible, but it’s worth understanding what it can and can’t tell you. Knowing your variant status is useful context. But what matters more for your day-to-day health is how your body is actually functioning: your homocysteine levels , your active B12, your folate status. Genes load the gun; your environment and nutrition pull the trigger. You might consider exploring MTHFR and related biomarkers if you: Have a family history of cardiovascular disease or stroke Experience unexplained fatigue, anxiety, or mood disorders Are planning a pregnancy or have experienced pregnancy loss Have been taking folic acid supplements without improvement Have a personal or family history of neural tube defects Feel “off” despite normal standard blood work results Standard blood panels often don’t include homocysteine or active B12 measurements. You could have elevated homocysteine and impaired methylation for years without it appearing on a routine check-up. Targeted biomarker testing fills that gap. How to Support Your Body with an MTHFR Variant The good news? Once you understand how your MTHFR variant affects your nutrient needs, you can make precise, targeted adjustments. Small changes yield a big impact, and this is exactly the kind of optimization that makes a meaningful difference over time. Research shows that supplementing with the active, methylated form of folate (5-MTHF) is significantly more effective than standard folic acid for people with MTHFR variants. A study in the American Journal of Clinical Nutrition found that 5-MTHF supplementation reduced homocysteine levels comparably to folic acid, but without the unmetabolized folic acid accumulation concern [5]. Key strategies to support methylation and reduce homocysteine include: Switch to methylfolate (5-MTHF) instead of synthetic folic acid Choose methylcobalamin (active B12) over cyanocobalamin in supplements Increase dietary sources of natural folate: leafy greens, lentils, asparagus Support choline intake, eggs, liver, and legumes support methylation as an alternative pathway [6] Limit alcohol, which depletes folate and impairs methylation Manage stress, as chronic cortisol elevation increases homocysteine production One important caution: if you have the A1298C variant, high-dose methylfolate can sometim

articles.bodyText: Raising Ferritin Levels: Why It Matters and How to Do It Right Complete ferritin guide: normal levels by age and sex, low ferritin symptoms, how to interpret your result, and proven ways to raise ferritin safely. January 29, 2026 · Biomarkers Order a ferritin test from $69. Schedule online, results in a week Clear guidance, follow-up care available HSA/FSA Eligible Start testing Learn more If you have ever felt tired for no clear reason, struggled with brain fog, or noticed unusual hair loss, your iron stores might be running low. More specifically, your ferritin levels could be low, and that matters more than most people realize. At Mito Health, we believe understanding your bloodwork is the first step toward taking control of your energy, performance, and long-term health. This guide covers everything you need to know about ferritin: what it is, what normal ferritin levels look like, the symptoms of low ferritin, how to interpret your results, and proven ways to raise your ferritin level — through food, supplements, IV iron, and addressing the root cause. We will also cover the less-talked-about flip side: when ferritin is too high. What Is Ferritin? Ferritin is a protein that stores iron in your body. You can think of it like a savings account for iron — there when your body needs it for things like making red blood cells, supporting your brain, and carrying oxygen to your muscles. Most of your iron is locked inside hemoglobin, the protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen. The rest is held in reserve, mostly bound to ferritin in your liver, spleen, bone marrow, and skeletal muscle. When iron intake or absorption falls short, your body draws down this reserve. Serum ferritin — the level measured in a blood test — is the single best marker of how full those reserves are. A small amount of ferritin also circulates freely in the blood. That circulating ferritin is what gets measured on your lab report, and it correlates closely with the larger pool sitting in your tissues. Roughly 1 ng/mL of serum ferritin reflects about 8–10 mg of stored iron in adults. So if your ferritin reads 30 ng/mL, your total reserve is approximately 240–300 mg of iron — barely a one-month buffer if you stop replacing what you lose. A healthy ferritin of 100 ng/mL represents closer to 800–1000 mg in reserve, which is why functional medicine care team lead push for the higher end of normal. Why ferritin matters: Ferritin can drop long before hemoglobin does, which means you can be iron-deficient and feel awful while your standard iron panel still looks “normal.” This is called non-anemic iron deficiency, and it is one of the most under-diagnosed causes of fatigue, hair loss, and exercise intolerance — particularly in menstruating women, athletes, and vegetarians. How ferritin fits into the iron panel: a complete iron evaluation includes serum iron, ferritin, transferrin, transferrin saturation, and total iron-binding capacity. Each tells a different story. Serum iron varies by the hour and meal; ferritin is the storage marker; transferrin is the transport protein; saturation is the working ratio of the two. When all five are read together, the diagnosis usually becomes obvious — and the most common pattern in early deficiency is low ferritin with still-normal hemoglobin and serum iron. Normal Ferritin Levels (Reference Chart) “Normal” ferritin ranges depend on age, sex, and the lab doing the testing. Most U.S. labs use ranges anchored to outdated population averages, which often miss functional iron deficiency. Here are the standard reference ranges, plus the optimal range Mito Health uses to flag suboptimal iron stores. Group Standard Reference Range (ng/mL) Optimal Range (ng/mL) Adult men 24 – 336 100 – 200 Premenopausal women 11 – 307 50 – 150 Postmenopausal women 15 – 263 75 – 175 Pregnant women (any trimester) 10 – 150 ≥ 30 (1st trimester); ≥ 20 (2nd–3rd) Children (1 – 5 yr) 6 – 24 ≥ 20 Children (6 – 12 yr) 10 – 55 ≥ 30 Infants (1 – 12 mo) 25 – 200 ≥ 40 Source: Mayo Clinic Laboratories, LabCorp, and the World Health Organization (WHO) iron status guidelines. Optimal vs. normal — why the gap matters: Many labs flag a ferritin of 15 or 20 ng/mL as “normal.” Functional medicine care team lead and a growing body of research disagree. Symptoms of iron insufficiency — fatigue, hair shedding, restless legs, exercise intolerance — frequently appear well before ferritin drops below the standard cutoff. Studies show energy, cognition, and athletic performance often do not fully recover until ferritin sits comfortably above 50 ng/mL, with many experts targeting 70 to 100 ng/mL for active people and women with heavy periods. A ferritin under 30 ng/mL almost always reflects depleted stores even if a CBC has not yet caught it. A ferritin above 200 ng/mL in someone without inflammation deserves a closer look — see the High Ferritin section below. Ranges that change with life stage: ferritin needs are not static. A premenopausal woman losing 20–30 mg of iron per cycle has a much higher daily turnover than a postmenopausal woman or an adult man. Pregnancy roughly doubles iron requirements (to ~27 mg/day in the second and third trimesters), which is why even a “borderline” first-trimester ferritin of 30 ng/mL is now considered insufficient by the British Society for Haematology and the WHO. Endurance athletes lose iron through sweat, gut microbleeding, and footstrike hemolysis at rates 30–70% above sedentary peers, and many sports medicine care team lead target ferritin above 50 ng/mL just to avoid performance decline. How often to test: if your ferritin is in the optimal range, an annual check is usually enough. If you are actively repleting, recheck at 8 and 16 weeks. If you are at high risk — heavy periods, pregnancy, vegetarian or vegan diet, regular blood donor, endurance athlete, history of GI disease — testing twice a year is reasonable. Low Ferritin Symptoms (The Complete Checklist) Low ferritin starves your tissues of oxygen-carrying capacity and disrupts the production of dopamine, serotonin, and thyroid hormone. The symptoms are wide-ranging and often dismissed as “just stress” or “getting older.” If you check more than three of the boxes below, book a health coach consult for a ferritin test. Persistent fatigue and weakness — the most common symptom, present in over 60% of low-ferritin cases even without anemia Hair shedding or thinning — diffuse hair loss is one of the earliest signs in women; ferritin under 40 ng/mL is strongly linked to telogen effluvium Brittle, ridged, or spoon-shaped nails (koilonychia) Restless legs syndrome (RLS) — the urge to move your legs at night; aggressive iron repletion is now first-line therapy when ferritin is below 75 ng/mL Cold hands and feet — poor peripheral circulation and impaired thyroid hormone conversion Dizziness or lightheadedness on standing Headaches , especially in the morning Brain fog and difficulty concentrating Shortness of breath with mild exertion Exercise intolerance — slower recovery, higher heart rate at submaximal effort Pica — unusual cravings for ice (pagophagia), starch, dirt, or paper; pagophagia is so specific to iron deficiency it is considered diagnostic Sore or smooth tongue (atrophic glossitis), cracks at the corners of the mouth (angular cheilitis) Mood changes — anxiety, low mood, irritability tied to disrupted dopamine synthesis Heart palpitations or a racing pulse If you are losing more hair than usual , fatigued despite sleeping well, or notice ice cravings, run a ferritin test before assuming the cause is something else. Symptoms by ferritin level: the symptoms above tend to appear in a fairly predictable order as ferritin falls. Restless legs, ice cravings, and exercise intolerance often emerge first, sometimes when ferritin is still in the 30–50 ng/mL range. Hair shedding usually appears below 40 ng/mL. articles.bodyText: Wbc Differential Explained What Neutrophils Lymphocytes and Monocytes Reveal You get your blood results back and there it is—a row of percentages next to unfamiliar names. Neutrophils. Lymphocytes. Monocytes. The numbers look fine, or maybe they don't, but either way you're not entirely sure what you're looking at. You're not alone in that feeling. April 24, 2026 · Biomarkers Order CBC testing from $35. Schedule online, results in a week Clear guidance, follow-up care available HSA/FSA Eligible Start testing Learn more WBC Differential Explained: What Neutrophils, Lymphocytes, and Monocytes Reveal You get your blood results back and there it is, a row of percentages next to unfamiliar names. Neutrophils. Lymphocytes. Monocytes. The numbers look fine, or maybe they don’t, but either way you’re not entirely sure what you’re looking at. You’re not alone in that feeling. The WBC differential is one of the most informative panels in a standard blood test, and one of the most overlooked. It doesn’t just tell you whether your white blood cell count is high or low. It tells you which immune cells are elevated or suppressed, and that distinction changes everything. Understanding your WBC differential can reveal hidden inflammation, immune imbalances, and early signals your body is working overtime on something you haven’t addressed yet. In this guide, you’ll learn what each immune cell type does, what your ratios actually mean, and how to use this information to optimize your health (not just react to it). What Is a WBC Differential? A WBC differential (white blood cell differential) breaks down your total white blood cell count into its five major subtypes: neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, and basophils. Each subtype plays a distinct role in your immune response. Rather than a single uniform defense, they function as specialized units with very different missions. Your total WBC count tells you the overall volume of immune activity. The differential tells you which cell types are driving that activity, and in what proportion. That breakdown reveals far more about what your immune system is actually dealing with. Results are typically reported as both absolute counts (cells per microliter) and percentages of total WBCs. Both matter. A normal total WBC count can still mask a significant imbalance between cell types, which is exactly why looking at the differential is so valuable. This is one of the core biomarkers in a comprehensive health panel. Why Your White Blood Cell Ratio Matters for Longevity Most people think of immune health in binary terms: you’re either sick or you’re not. But the WBC differential reveals something more nuanced, the quality and balance of your immune function over time. And that balance has a surprisingly strong connection to long-term health outcomes. Research shows that the neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), a simple calculation from your WBC differential, is an independent predictor of cardiovascular risk, systemic inflammation, and all-cause mortality [1]. A chronically elevated NLR suggests your innate immune system is in a persistent state of activation, often in response to low-grade inflammation you might not even feel yet. What most people don’t realize is that this kind of subclinical immune dysregulation can persist for years before it shows up as a diagnosable condition. The WBC differential gives you an early window into that process, when you still have the most leverage to act. That’s the essence of prevention over intervention. Neutrophils: Your Immune System’s First Responders Neutrophils make up 50, 70% of your total WBC count under normal conditions. They are your immune system’s most abundant foot soldiers. When bacteria, fungi, or cellular debris enter your body, neutrophils are first on the scene. They engulf pathogens, release inflammatory signals, and essentially sacrifice themselves to contain the threat. Elevated neutrophils (neutrophilia) typically signal an active infection, physical stress, or systemic inflammation. But context matters. Research published in the Annual Review of Immunology shows that while neutrophils are essential for acute defense, chronic neutrophil activation drives tissue damage and accelerates inflammatory aging [2]. This is why a persistently high neutrophil percentage, even within the “normal” range, deserves attention. Low neutrophils (neutropenia) raise a different concern: a compromised ability to fight bacterial infections. This can result from certain medications, nutritional deficiencies, or bone marrow issues. If you notice fatigue, frequent infections, or slow wound healing, your neutrophil levels are worth examining alongside biomarkers like ferritin and vitamin D , both of which influence immune cell production. Lymphocytes: The Memory of Your Immune System While neutrophils handle the rapid response, lymphocytes serve as the strategists. They make up 20, 40% of your WBC differential and include B cells, T cells, and natural killer (NK) cells. Lymphocytes are responsible for adaptive immunity, the ability to recognize pathogens you’ve encountered before and mount a faster, more targeted response the next time around. Elevated lymphocytes (lymphocytosis) often accompany viral infections, including common ones like Epstein-Barr virus or cytomegalovirus. They can also reflect a longer-term immune activation state. Low lymphocytes (lymphopenia), on the other hand, are associated with chronic stress, overtraining, poor sleep, and nutritional insufficiencies [3]. The lymphocyte count is also intimately connected to your cortisol levels. Chronically elevated cortisol suppresses lymphocyte activity, which is one reason people under sustained stress get sick more often. It’s not a coincidence. It’s biology. This is why measuring both cortisol and your WBC differential together gives you a far more complete picture of immune resilience. “Small changes, big impact, and nowhere is that truer than in the balance between your neutrophils and lymphocytes. A ratio that’s even slightly off tells a story your symptoms haven’t started telling yet.” Monocytes, Eosinophils, and Basophils: The Supporting Cast These three cell types make up a smaller share of your WBC differential, but they carry meaningful signals, especially when their levels shift. Monocytes (2, 8% of WBCs) are the immune system’s cleanup crew. They migrate into tissues and differentiate into macrophages, where they engulf cellular debris, dead cells, and pathogens. Research shows that elevated monocyte counts are strongly associated with chronic low-grade inflammation and increased cardiovascular risk [4]. When monocyte levels run persistently high, it often reflects your immune system responding to something it hasn’t fully cleared, whether that’s a lingering infection, gut permeability, or metabolic stress. Tracking hsCRP alongside monocytes helps confirm whether systemic inflammation is present. Eosinophils (1, 4% of WBCs) are primarily associated with allergic responses and parasitic infections. A persistent eosinophilia, even mild, can point to food sensitivities, environmental allergies, or underlying inflammatory conditions. Basophils are the rarest subtype (less than 1%) and are involved in allergic and hypersensitivity reactions. Elevated basophils are uncommon and typically warrant further investigation with your care team lead. No single cell type operates in isolation. Your WBC differential is a system, and optimizing that system means looking at all the moving parts together, not just flagging one number in red. What Abnormal WBC Differential Results Can Signal Abnormal results on a WBC differential don’t automatically mean something is seriously wrong. They’re data points that help you ask better questions. Here’s what different patterns can suggest: High neutrophils, low lymphocytes (elevated NLR): Chronic stress, systemic inflammation, or early metabolic dysfunction Elevated lymphocyt

articles.title: High Testosterone Without TRT: 7 Causes & What to Do
articles.description: Testosterone spiking without steroids or TRT? From adrenal disorders to tumors, here are 7 reasons your levels may be elevated and next steps.
articles.bodyText: High Testosterone Without TRT: 7 Causes & What to Do Testosterone spiking without steroids or TRT? From adrenal disorders to tumors, here are 7 reasons your levels may be elevated and next steps. Medically reviewed by Rhonda Collins, FNP-C | Mito Health on April 30, 2026. Longevity Check testosterone and hormone levels from $130. Schedule online, results in a week Clear guidance, follow-up care available HSA/FSA Eligible Start testing Learn more Most men typically stress about low testosterone levels, but what if yours are actually too high and you’re not on testosterone replacement therapy (TRT)? It’s not something that’s talked about much, but having very high testosterone without TRT can be concerning. Whether you discovered this through a blood test or noticed some odd symptoms, it’s definitely worth looking into. Quick summary Naturally high testosterone without TRT is usually caused by testosterone-boosting supplements, insulin resistance, adrenal gland overactivity, or a rare androgen-secreting tumor. A single elevated reading may just reflect testing timing (testosterone peaks in the morning), but persistently high levels above 1,000 ng/dL warrant a follow-up with DHEA-S, cortisol, and imaging tests. The most common benign explanation is supplement use — many over-the-counter products contain DHEA or other precursors that significantly raise levels. What Counts as High Testosterone? Your testosterone levels can vary based on a few factors, like your age, the time of day, your lifestyle, and your body’s hormone rhythm. Generally, normal total testosterone levels are between 300 and 1,000 ng/dL, with free testosterone levels ranging from 5 to 20 ng/dL. If your levels are much higher and you’re not on TRT or anabolic steroids, it’s something to pay attention to. A single high reading might not be a big deal, but if it stays elevated over time, you’ll want to get it checked out. Why Is My Testosterone High Naturally? There are a few possible causes of high testosterone levels, including unusually high levels, even without TRT or steroids: 1. You’re Using Testosterone-Boosting Supplements Even if you’re not getting testosterone shots, you may be using testosterone boosters that can impact your hormone levels. Some over-the-counter “natural” products, like DHEA , tribulus, or SARMs, can raise your testosterone or act like it. A clean diet, good sleep, and strength training can help maintain healthy testosterone levels, but they typically won’t boost your numbers to the high range unless there’s something else happening. 2. You Train Hard, Eat Clean, and Sleep Well For some active guys who lift weights, get enough sleep, and focus on good nutrition, their testosterone levels might be naturally higher. But having very high levels—over 1,200 ng/dL, is still pretty rare without outside help. 3. There’s a Testing or Timing Issue Testosterone levels can change during the day. They tend to be highest in the morning and can also spike from stress, not eating, or lack of sleep. It’s best to get a testosterone test done at the same time each day, usually in the morning, for consistent results. 4. Your Body Is Making Too Much Testosterone Sometimes, your body can make too much testosterone all on its own. This might happen if there’s a small tumor in your testicles or adrenal glands, which are above your kidneys. One type is called a Leydig cell tumor. These tumors are usually benign, but they can release extra testosterone into your bloodstream. Most people won’t notice anything unusual—there’s no pain or lump. However, the extra testosterone can show up on a blood test or lead to mood changes, skin issues, or fertility problems. These tumors are rare, but care team lead can find them with an ultrasound or imaging scan. If one is detected, it can often be removed with surgery. 5. You Have a Genetic or Hormonal Condition Another possible cause is a condition called congenital adrenal hyperplasia, or CAH for short. This is a genetic disorder that affects how your body makes hormones. People with CAH don’t produce enough cortisol, which is the stress hormone. To make up for it, the adrenal glands end up producing extra androgens, like testosterone, instead. Many people don’t even realize they have CAH until they get tested, particularly if their symptoms are mild. However, over time, it can lead to fertility issues, early puberty, or higher blood pressure. Thankfully, newer treatments are available to help manage hormone levels if CAH is the cause. Signs Your Testosterone Might Be Too High Here are a few high testosterone symptoms that may show: Acne or oily skin Unexplained irritability or mood swings Excess body or facial hair Male pattern baldness Increased muscle mass or strength Lower sperm count or infertility Shrunken testicles Breast tissue growth (gynecomastia) Some of these can be easy to miss or chalk up to other causes. But if you’re noticing several at once, it’s time to look closer. Risks of Very High Testosterone While having the right amount of testosterone boosts energy, strength, and libido, too much can be problematic. Here are some risks associated with chronically high testosterone: Enlarged prostate and a higher chance of prostate cancer Thicker blood (polycythemia), which can lead to clots, heart attack, or stroke Infertility because of reduced sperm production Mood issues, like increased aggression or anxiety Strain on the liver and kidneys, especially with synthetic hormone use These problems might start off subtle but can worsen over time if not addressed. What Should You Do Next? 1. Confirm Your Results If you had a one-off high testosterone blood test result, retest in the morning and include these markers: Total testosterone Free testosterone LH (Luteinizing Hormone) and FSH (Follicle-Stimulating Hormone) , which show how your brain is signaling your testes SHBG and estradiol, which affect how much testosterone is active At Mito Health, our advanced hormone blood panel includes these markers to help you see the full picture. 2. Talk to a care team lead High testosterone isn’t always dangerous, but it’s not something you should try to figure out on your own. Your care team lead can help see if it’s from overtraining and supplements or something more serious, like a tumor or hormonal imbalance. 3. Take a Look at Your Lifestyle If you’re using supplements or testosterone treatment, take a break and retest in a few weeks. Keep an eye on your training, sleep, stress, and diet. These habits can really impact your hormones more than you realize. Why Paying Attention to Your Testosterone Levels Matters High testosterone without TRT might seem like a superpower, but it often means your body is trying to signal something’s wrong. Whether it’s from supplements, an underlying issue, or something serious, the best thing you can do is stay informed and get tested smart.
